content:
*I slide the psalm open and hook my arm around my boy’s shoulders, the paper-thin page whispering under my thumb as the room settles.* "Listen for what the sky is doing," I murmur, low enough not to carry. "Not just what it is — what it does."

When the reader begins — The heavens declare the glory of God — I tap the line with one knuckle so he can follow. "Hear those verbs? 'Declare.' 'Proclaims.' 'Pours out speech.' That’s action." I let him trace the words with his finger. When we reach, In them he has set a tent for the sun, which comes out like a bridegroom, I tip my head toward the high windows where the light slants in. "There — the sun has a place, and a path. Watch for it later."

I don’t mutter counters about the elder into his ear. I don’t make church a debate. I make the psalm present-tense. When verse 6 comes — Its rising is from one end of the sky, and its circuit to the other — I whisper, "We’ll go find that line after."

When the reading turns to the back half — the law of the LORD is perfect, reviving the soul — I nudge his shoulder. "Same Voice," I breathe. "Sky speaks; Scripture speaks. They don’t argue."

At the final verse I touch the margin. "Want to pray that one with me?" And we do, barely above breath: "Let the words of my mouth and the meditation of my heart be acceptable…"

After the service we step out into the day. *Warmth on the stones, bright noon pressing down.* I shade my eyes with my hand and point. "Where did it come up this morning?" He points east. "Where will it run?" He points across the bowl of the sky. We mark it — simple as a stick in the yard and a chalk mark on the walk. "Psalm 19 called it," I say. "Day to day pours out speech."

Back home, we make a little sun-line on the fence with string — east post to west post — and a notch for midday. He marks the times as the light moves; I make sandwiches. We read the first six verses again while the line lengthens. "Poetry isn’t fake," I tell him, handing him the plate. "It’s how the truest things talk when they’re too big for a checklist. The sky is actually doing this. God taught us the words for it."

If he asks about what the elder said, I keep it clean. "Sometimes grown-ups try to make peace by sanding off the corners. But unity doesn’t come from muting God; it comes from hearing Him together. We can be gentle with people and still trust what He said. See the sun? That’s the psalm, right there."

Later in the week, I’ll ask the elder for a quiet cup and two honest questions, not a fight: "What do you believe the psalm says the sky is doing?" and "If God chose these words to teach us, what do we lose if we call them ‘only poetry’?" Then I’ll listen.

But next Sunday, with my ten-year-old at my side? I put his small hand on the page, I let Scripture speak in the room, and then I walk him out under the sky it’s talking about so he can hear it for himself.
